legal: For freelance contracts, always include scope of work, payment terms, deadlines, and intellectual property clauses. Consider using templates from platforms like LegalZoom or consulting a local attorney for complex projects.
When dealing with international clients, be aware of tax implications. Many countries have tax treaties - check if yours does. Consider registering for an EIN or equivalent business number.
Always get agreements in writing. A simple contract protects both parties. Include cancellation policies, revision limits, and dispute resolution methods.
pricing: For hourly rates, research your market on platforms like Glassdoor, Upwork, and Toptal. Junior developers typically charge $25-50/hr, mid-level $50-100/hr, and senior $100-200+/hr depending on specialization.
Consider value-based pricing for projects where you can clearly demonstrate ROI. If your work will generate $100,000 for the client, charging $10,000-20,000 is reasonable.
Don't forget to account for non-billable hours (admin, marketing, learning). Most freelancers should add 25-40% to their base rate to cover these costs and maintain profitability.
startup: Start by validating your idea with a Minimum Viable Product (MVP). Focus on solving one core problem extremely well before expanding. Use tools like Landing Page tests and customer interviews.

For early-stage funding, consider bootstrapping, friends & family, angel investors, or platforms like Y Combinator and Techstars. Prepare a clear pitch deck with problem, solution, market size, and traction.
Build in public! Share your journey on Twitter/X, LinkedIn, and indie hacker communities. This builds trust, attracts early users, and can lead to valuable connections and feedback.
career: Build a strong portfolio showcasing 3-5 best projects. Include case studies with problem statements, your approach, technologies used, and measurable outcomes.
Networking is crucial. Attend meetups, contribute to open source, and engage on developer communities. Many job opportunities come through referrals rather than job boards.
Keep learning continuously. Dedicate 20% of your time to learning new skills. Follow industry leaders, take courses on platforms like Udemy, Coursera, and Frontend Masters.
general: I'm GigZora's AI Assistant! I can help with legal advice for freelancers, pricing strategies, startup guidance, and career tips. Try asking about any of these topics!
Need help getting started? Try asking me about: 'How should I price my services?', 'What legal protections do I need?', or 'How do I start a freelance business?'
Remember, I provide general guidance based on common freelancing best practices. For specific legal or financial advice, always consult with qualified professionals.
